challenges.title: Lambda Sorter
challenges.description: Given `pairs = [(2, 'b'), (1, 'a'), (3, 'c')]`, sort the list by the second element of each tuple using a `lambda` function. Store the result as `sorted_pairs`.
challenges.starter_code:
pairs = [(2, 'b'), (1, 'a'), (3, 'c')]

# Sort pairs by the second element using a lambda
sorted_pairs = None  # Replace with your code

print(sorted_pairs)

challenges.title: Apply Chain
challenges.description: Write a function `apply_all(value, *funcs)` that takes an initial value and any number of functions, then applies each function in sequence to the result of the previous one. Return the final result.
challenges.starter_code:
def apply_all(value, *funcs):
    # Your code here
    pass

# Test your function
print(apply_all(5, str))
print(apply_all(-3, abs, str))
print(apply_all("hello", str.upper, list))
challenges.test_code:
assert apply_all(5, str) == "5"
assert apply_all(-3, abs, str) == "3"
assert apply_all("hello", str.upper, list) == ['H', 'E', 'L', 'L', 'O']

challenges.title: Recursive Flatten
challenges.description: Write a function `flatten(lst)` that takes a nested list (e.g., `[1, [2, [3, 4]], 5]`) and returns a single flat list containing all elements in order (e.g., `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]`). Use recursion to handle arbitrary nesting depth.
challenges.starter_code:
def flatten(lst):
    # Your code here
    pass

# Test your function
print(flatten([1, [2, [3, 4]], 5]))
print(flatten([1, 2, 3]))
print(flatten([[1], [2], [3]]))
